title: Introduction
text: Before we jump into projections and hands-on activities, let's take a moment to map out the landscape of algorithms. GDS offers a wide array of algorithms, neatly organized into five categories. Each of these categories is designed to answer a specific type of question. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-3-algorithm-categories-s036-000-new] As you can see in this diagram, the categories include Centrality, Community Detection, Similarity, Pathfinding, and Embeddings. Each one is tailored to address different analytical needs.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll have the skills to identify the five categories of GDS algorithms and understand their purposes. You'll be able to match analytical questions to the right algorithm category, recognize that while algorithms compute scores, interpreting those scores requires domain knowledge, and understand the basic graph requirements for different types of algorithms.

title: The Five Categories
text: Let's break down the five categories. First, we have Centrality, which helps us determine which nodes are most important. Examples include PageRank, Degree, and Betweenness. Next is Community Detection, which identifies groups within the data, using algorithms like Louvain, Leiden, and WCC. Then there's Similarity, which finds nodes that are alike, with Node Similarity and KNN as examples. Pathfinding is all about finding the best route, using algorithms like Dijkstra and Yen's K-Shortest. Finally, Node Embeddings help represent data for machine learning, with FastRP and Node2Vec leading the way.

title: Choosing a Category
text: When you're starting out, begin with your question. If you're asking, 'Who matters most?' then Centrality is your go-to. For questions like 'What groups exist?' you'll want Community Detection. If you're looking for similarities, turn to Similarity. For route questions like 'How do I get from A to B?' Pathfinding is key. And if you're thinking about machine learning, Embeddings is the category for you. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-3-algorithm-categories-s039-003-new] This flowchart helps guide you from your analytical question to the right algorithm category, illustrating the decision-making process.

title: Algorithms Are Tools
text: It's important to remember that algorithms are tools. They don't 'find' fraud or 'discover' communities on their own. What they do is compute scores and assignments based on the graph's structure. It's up to you to interpret these results. Whether a cluster indicates a fraud ring, a department, or just noise depends entirely on your domain knowledge. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-3-algorithm-categories-s040-004-new] This illustration shows how algorithms compute scores and require human interpretation, with examples of different interpretations based on domain knowledge.

title: Algorithm Requirements
text: Some algorithms come with specific graph requirements. For instance, the Leiden algorithm requires undirected relationships, while Pathfinding typically needs weighted relationships. Node Similarity works best on bipartite graphs. You'll learn how to configure projections to meet these requirements. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-3-algorithm-categories-s041-005-new] This diagram illustrates the different graph requirements for specific algorithms, such as undirected relationships for Leiden and weighted relationships for Pathfinding.

title: What's Ahead
text: In the upcoming lessons, you'll dive deeper into creating projections, understanding how graph structure affects algorithms, and getting hands-on practice with each category. It's going to be an exciting journey!

title: Lesson Summary
text: To wrap up, GDS algorithms are categorized into five main groups: Centrality for ranking importance, Community Detection for finding groups, Similarity for comparing nodes, Pathfinding for finding routes, and Node Embeddings for creating vectors for machine learning. The key to choosing the right category lies in your analytical question.
